episode_meta.title: Florent Daudens: How Open-Source AI Puts Newsrooms Back in the Driver's Seat

use_cases.description: Confidential transcription of interviews using Whisper running locally on a journalist's computer with speaker diarization, ensuring no data is sent to external servers
use_cases.outcome: Privacy-first transcription with speaker identification, no external data transmission

use_cases.description: Scraping websites without coding by prompting an AI model with natural language and a URL to retrieve and structure press releases from government websites
use_cases.outcome: Automated retrieval and structuring of web content without coding skills

use_cases.description: Image editing using natural language prompts instead of Photoshop, including removing objects and their shadows for social media thumbnails
use_cases.tool_used: Open-source image editing model (Essential AI Toolkit)
use_cases.outcome: High-quality image editing without Photoshop expertise; removes objects and shadows seamlessly

use_cases.description: Handwriting recognition (OCR) on difficult-to-read documents such as US presidential transition letters, achieving 100% accuracy
use_cases.outcome: 100% accuracy on handwriting that is difficult even for humans to decipher

use_cases.description: Video shot listing using a small vision language model running locally, extracting and describing every shot in a video in minutes
use_cases.outcome: Complete video shot list generated in minutes locally, a task that previously took hours in TV stations

use_cases.description: Extracting data from charts and rebuilding it in spreadsheets
use_cases.outcome: Structured data extraction from visual charts

use_cases.description: Optimizing headlines for SEO using AI
use_cases.outcome: Improved SEO performance of news headlines

use_cases.description: Building prototype apps on the fly using LLMs (vibe coding), enabling non-coders to create audience-facing solutions through conversational prompting
use_cases.outcome: Rapid prototyping of apps without coding knowledge; can iterate and add functions through conversation

use_cases.description: Building a shared prompt library where newsroom staff iterate on prompts collaboratively, treating prompts like code
use_cases.outcome: Common prompt library accessible to entire newsroom; collaborative improvement of prompts creates organizational value

use_cases.description: Improving search engines with conversational AI by plugging content databases into chatbots using embedding models for better retrieval accuracy
use_cases.outcome: More accurate conversational search over news archives

use_cases.description: Using an AI agent with Browser Use to autonomously browse BBC website, find an article about the Oscars, read it, and produce a summary
use_cases.outcome: Agent successfully browsed, found, read, and summarized news content autonomously

use_cases.description: Embedding small text-to-speech models in news apps so users can switch from reading to listening mid-article on their phones
use_cases.outcome: On-device, on-demand audio conversion of articles

use_cases.description: CNN laying off 200 broadcast staff and hiring 200 people in data science and development to pivot toward AI-powered digital experiences
use_cases.outcome: Major organizational restructuring toward AI-powered content delivery as broadcast audience declines

use_cases.description: AI-powered newsroom analytics that go beyond dashboards to understand user intent, personalize content journeys, and provide actionable editorial recommendations via agents
use_cases.outcome: Agents that understand user journeys, suggest editorial improvements, and report on actions taken; surpasses static dashboards

key_claims.claim: Hugging Face has 7 million users and over 1.5 million models, hundreds of thousands of datasets, and AI app spaces
key_claims.speaker: Florent Daudens

key_claims.claim: Over 2,000 community models have been built on top of DeepSeek R1 on Hugging Face
key_claims.speaker: Florent Daudens

key_claims.claim: DeepSeek R1's largest model is over 600 billion parameters, but distilled versions down to ~7B parameters can run on a MacBook with 16GB RAM
key_claims.speaker: Florent Daudens

key_claims.claim: Llama 3.2 was 400B+ parameters; six months later a 70B parameter model delivered better performance, cutting size by 5x
key_claims.speaker: Florent Daudens

key_claims.claim: SmallVLM 2, a vision language model at only 250 million parameters, can shot-list an entire video in a few minutes running locally
key_claims.speaker: Florent Daudens

key_claims.claim: Each image generation request to ChatGPT is equivalent in energy to a full iPhone charge, according to Hugging Face researcher Sasha Luccioni
key_claims.speaker: Florent Daudens

key_claims.claim: About 80% of newsroom staff either don't care about AI, don't have time for it, or are afraid of it, creating a two-tier divide
key_claims.speaker: Florent Daudens

key_claims.claim: With open-source AI, you can switch models in one line of code, avoiding vendor lock-in
key_claims.speaker: Florent Daudens

key_claims.claim: Perplexity captures 12 words per query versus Google's 3 words, providing much richer user intent data
key_claims.speaker: Florent Daudens

key_claims.claim: ChatGPT only searches and pulls from news publishers it has licensing deals with, creating an information access divide
key_claims.speaker: Nikita Roy

key_claims.claim: NotebookLM and Perplexity are innovations at the intersection of tech and journalism that could have come from newsrooms
key_claims.speaker: Florent Daudens

key_claims.claim: CNN laid off approximately 200 people in broadcast and hired 200 in data science and development due to audience shifts
key_claims.speaker: Florent Daudens

key_claims.claim: Hugging Face's CEO Clem said they launch approximately 500 innovations; maybe 5 change the company's trajectory
key_claims.speaker: Florent Daudens

key_claims.claim: Hugging Face has a zero-meeting policy with approximately 250 distributed employees worldwide
key_claims.speaker: Florent Daudens

key_claims.claim: The Financial Times treats prompts the same way they treat code, with a shared prompt library iterated on collaboratively
key_claims.speaker: Florent Daudens

challenges.description: Two-tier divide in newsrooms: approximately 20% are engaged with AI while 80% either don't care, lack time, or fear AI replacement
Lack of internal technical skills in newsrooms to harness open-source AI, leading to over-reliance on large foundational models for tasks that smaller specialized models could handle
Environmental cost of AI: each ChatGPT image generation equals a full iPhone charge; massive energy consumption threatens power grids
Copyright and training data concerns: news organizations worry about their content being used without consent for model training
AI agents will be able to browse news websites, extract content, and bypass UX/UI and ads entirely, threatening ad-based revenue models
Google AI Overviews, Perplexity, and ChatGPT are diverting traffic from news publishers, undermining the search-to-article ad impression revenue model
Editorial control is at risk as AI intermediates between newsrooms and audiences, enabling users to consume content in ways not controlled by publishers
Information access divide: ChatGPT only surfaces content from licensed publishers, excluding smaller publishers who cannot afford licensing deals
Newsroom leadership often fails to create adequate space and resources for AI experimentation, stifling innovation from younger staff
Pending lawsuits on fair use/fair dealing need resolution before the legal landscape around AI and news content is clear
News avoidance may partly stem from content not being personalized or conversational enough to meet diverse user needs

quotes.text: one of the key missions of Hugging Face is is basically fighting the concentration of power. We want people to be able to choose how and what they want to use in AI.
quotes.speaker: Florent Daudens
quotes.pedagogical_value: Encapsulates the core philosophy driving open-source AI as a counterforce to Big Tech concentration, directly relevant to newsroom autonomy

quotes.text: you has to make AI your own because it's not that complicated, especially because you know when you look at it, what is AI basically? It's the ability to talk to a machine with your own words.
quotes.speaker: Florent Daudens
quotes.pedagogical_value: Demystifies AI for non-technical journalists; reframes AI as a communication skill rather than a technical one

quotes.text: we're seeing more and more a two-tier approach inside of newsrooms and inside of the industry because you have some people who are really, really interested in AI. And I would say maybe 80% of the people are like either they don't care or they don't have time or they're afraid of AI.
quotes.speaker: Florent Daudens
quotes.pedagogical_value: Quantifies the adoption divide within newsrooms; useful for framing the urgency of AI literacy programs

quotes.text: Perplexity without news is like an arm less, you know. But I think that raises a very important point. And a reason why that hasn't happened is because of what perplexity does is basically letting the user, the audience, be in the driver's seat about how they're going to be interacting with that content.
quotes.speaker: Florent Daudens and Nikita Roy
quotes.topic: AI products built on news content
quotes.pedagogical_value: Highlights the paradox that AI products depend on journalism yet were not built by newsrooms; illustrates missed product innovation opportunity

quotes.text: the future of media and journalism isn't just about owning the content, it's about owning the experience. It's about the inference, the interaction, the integrations that you bring into your experience that's actually might end up being more valuable than even page views were ever.
quotes.speaker: Nikita Roy
quotes.pedagogical_value: Frames a strategic paradigm shift from content ownership to experience ownership as the key to newsroom sustainability

quotes.text: innovate faster and and you know don't fall in love with your innovations, that's fundamental.
quotes.speaker: Florent Daudens
quotes.pedagogical_value: Distills the tech industry's ship-fast-and-iterate mindset into actionable advice for newsroom leaders

recommendations.advice: Ask whether AI is truly a better solution than what you are already doing before deploying it; not every task requires AI
recommendations.speaker: Florent Daudens

recommendations.advice: Use small, specialized open-source models instead of large foundational models for specific tasks to reduce cost, environmental impact, and vendor dependency
recommendations.speaker: Florent Daudens

recommendations.advice: Build ongoing AI training and conversation into daily newsroom workflows rather than relying on one-off training sessions scheduled months in advance
recommendations.speaker: Florent Daudens

recommendations.advice: Run open-source AI models locally for sensitive or confidential journalism work to maintain privacy and avoid sending data to external servers
recommendations.speaker: Florent Daudens

recommendations.advice: Treat prompts like code: build shared prompt libraries that the entire newsroom can access, iterate on, and improve collaboratively, following the Financial Times model
recommendations.speaker: Florent Daudens

recommendations.advice: Leverage open-source courses, tutorials, communities, and no-code solutions as entry points for learning AI; join communities like Daudens' Slack workspace for journalists building with AI
recommendations.speaker: Florent Daudens

recommendations.advice: Newsroom leadership must proactively create space for AI experimentation, provide resources (especially time), and show concrete examples of AI value to combat fear and disengagement
recommendations.speaker: Florent Daudens

recommendations.advice: Prepare for AI agents that will browse and extract news content without visiting your UX; think about how your content and experience will be consumed by machines, not just humans
recommendations.speaker: Florent Daudens

recommendations.advice: Focus on building new revenue streams around inference (real-time content delivery, on-demand video/ad generation, personalization) rather than relying solely on traditional ad-impression models
recommendations.speaker: Florent Daudens

recommendations.advice: Think of news as a conversation to combat news avoidance; conversational AI can make news less intimidating and allow users to ask questions they would not ask when reading a static article
recommendations.speaker: Florent Daudens
recommendations.target_audience: product teams and audience engagement leaders

recommendations.advice: Innovate faster, ship quickly, and don't fall in love with your innovations; adopt a product mindset from the tech industry where you launch many experiments and keep the few that work
recommendations.speaker: Florent Daudens

recommendations.advice: Consider embedding small AI models directly into news apps (e.g., text-to-speech) to offer on-device personalized experiences
recommendations.speaker: Florent Daudens
recommendations.target_audience: news app developers and product teams

Florent Daudens' Slack workspace for journalists
